currency.name: Jordanian Dinar
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 10 JOD (the Jordanian Dinar is strong, things feel expensive)

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Jordan on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the Middle East. Jordan borders Israel, the Palestinian territories, Syria, Iraq and Saudi Arabia 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Jordan?
mapQuestions.answer: Amman, built on seven (now many more) hills in the north of the country. About half of all Jordanians live in or around it.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Jordan speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Arabic. Most Jordanians who work in tourism, business or government also speak good English.

mapQuestions.question: What is the Dead Sea?
mapQuestions.answer: A salt lake shared with Israel, 430 metres BELOW sea level (the lowest point on land anywhere on Earth). The water is so salty you float without trying.

mapQuestions.question: What is Petra?
mapQuestions.answer: An ancient city carved straight into pink-red sandstone cliffs, built by the Nabataean people over 2,000 years ago. One of the New 7 Wonders of the World.

foods.name: Mansaf
foods.description: Jordan's national dish: lamb cooked in a tangy fermented-yogurt sauce ("jameed"), served on a giant platter of rice with flatbread underneath, topped with toasted almonds and pine nuts. Eaten communally with the right hand.

foods.name: Falafel
foods.description: Deep-fried balls of mashed chickpeas and herbs, served in flatbread with tahini, salad and pickles. The standard Jordanian breakfast and street snack.

foods.name: Hummus and ful
foods.description: Hummus (chickpea-tahini dip) and "ful medames" (slow-cooked fava beans with lemon, garlic and olive oil). Eaten with hot pita straight from the oven.

foods.name: Maqluba
foods.description: "Upside-down" rice: meat, fried aubergine, cauliflower, potato and rice cooked together in one pot, then FLIPPED onto a serving plate so the layers stand like a cake.

foods.name: Knafeh
foods.description: A hot sweet dessert of soft white cheese under a layer of crispy orange shredded pastry, soaked in rose-water syrup. The most famous Levantine sweet.

foods.name: Bedouin tea
foods.description: Strong black tea boiled with sugar and fresh sage or mint leaves, poured into small glasses around a desert fire. Hospitality in a cup.

scavengerItems.label: The Treasury façade at Petra, carved into pink sandstone
A camel and Bedouin guide in Wadi Rum desert
Floating in the Dead Sea (salty water makes you bob like a cork)
Red Wadi Rum sand dunes (also a Star Wars / Dune / Martian filming location)
A small cup of strong Bedouin coffee or sage tea
A blue-tiled mosque dome in Amman
Roman ruins at Jerash (one of the best preserved Roman cities anywhere)
A horse trotting through the Siq (the long canyon entrance to Petra)
Limestone rocks carved with Nabataean tombs and façades
A black desert sky FULL of stars in Wadi Rum
Coral reefs off Aqaba (Jordan's tiny stretch of Red Sea coast)
A Bedouin tent with carpets and tea kettles around a fire

geoMatches.description: The capital, built on hills in the north. Roman amphitheatre in the centre, a hilltop Citadel with views over the city, and one of the friendliest big-city scenes in the region.
The ancient Nabataean city of rose-coloured stone. The famous Treasury façade, reached through a 1.2 km narrow canyon called the Siq, is one of the most photographed places on Earth.
A red sand desert with towering sandstone mountains. Famous as the filming location for Lawrence of Arabia, parts of Star Wars, Dune, The Martian and many more.
The lowest point on land anywhere on Earth, 430 m below sea level. So salty (10x saltier than the ocean) that you float without trying.
One of the best-preserved Roman provincial cities anywhere. Colonnaded streets, theatres, temples and a great oval forum, in the green hills north of Amman.
Jordan's only sea port, on the Red Sea. World-class coral-reef diving and warm winter beaches.

senses.smell: Cardamom in Bedouin coffee, hot olive oil in a falafel shop, sage tea steaming over a desert fire, rose water in syrup-soaked knafeh, the dry warm smell of Wadi Rum sand…
senses.hear: The call to prayer drifting across Amman at sunset, Arabic greetings ("Marhaba!" "Ahlan!"), the silence of the Wadi Rum desert at night, hooves on Petra's old stones, oud music in a restaurant…
senses.taste: Tangy mansaf with jameed yogurt, fluffy fresh pita, sweet honeyed knafeh, salty Dead Sea water (you WILL taste it), strong sweet Bedouin tea…
senses.feel: Hot desert sand under bare feet, the strange weightless float of the Dead Sea, smooth carved sandstone in Petra, soft Bedouin wool rugs, the cool of a stone Amman house at midday…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Who carved Petra?
stories.body: Petra was the capital city of the Nabataeans, a desert Arab people who controlled the spice and incense caravan routes from Arabia to the Mediterranean from about 300 BC. They were rich enough to carve an entire city directly into pink sandstone cliffs, with palaces, tombs, temples and a 7,000-seat theatre, all cut by hand into the rock. They also engineered an incredible water system, channels and tanks that brought reliable water across the dry hills. The city was finally absorbed into the Roman Empire in 106 AD, slowly emptied over the centuries, and was hidden from the outside world until a Swiss explorer, Johann Burckhardt, found it again in 1812. Today it is one of Jordan's biggest visitor draws and a UNESCO site.

stories.question: Why does the Dead Sea make you float?
stories.body: The Dead Sea is about TEN times saltier than the ocean. Salt water is denser than fresh water, so the saltier it gets, the more easily things float in it. The Dead Sea is so dense you literally can't sink. People famously read newspapers floating on the surface for tourist photos. The salt comes from millions of years of rivers running into the basin (it is the lowest point on land, 430 m below sea level) without ever flowing back out, evaporating in the sun and leaving the minerals behind. The Dead Sea is also shrinking fast, about a metre per year, because so much water is now taken from the Jordan River for farming and drinking before it can reach the sea.
stories.thinkingQuestion: The Dead Sea is shrinking a metre every year. What would it mean if it disappeared completely in our lifetime?

stories.question: Who were the Bedouin?
stories.body: The Bedouin are nomadic Arab peoples who have crossed the deserts of Arabia, Jordan, Egypt, Iraq and beyond for thousands of years, living in goat-hair tents and herding camels, goats and sheep. Many Bedouin families still live in Wadi Rum, though now in mixed traditional-modern ways: tents and 4x4s, camel rides and mobile phones, tea kettles and solar panels. Bedouin hospitality has its own rules: a stranger arriving at your tent is offered three small cups of coffee, sugar-free, in a specific order, and is your guest for three days and four nights with no questions asked. You can sleep in a Bedouin camp at the foot of the Wadi Rum cliffs.

stories.question: Why is there a Roman city in Jordan?
stories.body: The Roman Empire at its peak reached all the way to here. Jerash (called "Gerasa" in Roman times) was a wealthy provincial city, growing on trade routes between Damascus and Petra. It has colonnaded streets, two big theatres, a giant oval forum (rare in Roman architecture), temples to Zeus and Artemis, and triumphal arches, all still standing today. A massive earthquake in 749 AD destroyed much of it, and the ruins were buried under sand for centuries before being excavated in the 1800s. Today Jerash is one of the best-preserved Roman cities anywhere outside of Italy. You can run down a 2,000-year-old chariot track.

stories.question: How did Wadi Rum become a movie star?
stories.body: Wadi Rum is so unearthly that filmmakers use it whenever they need a planet that isn't Earth. The 1962 epic "Lawrence of Arabia" was filmed here. So was 2015's "The Martian" (where Wadi Rum stood in for Mars). Recent Star Wars episodes were filmed here. So was "Dune" (Wadi Rum doubled for the desert planet Arrakis). Whenever you see an unbelievable red desert canyon in a sci-fi or historical movie, there is a good chance it is Wadi Rum. The Bedouin community here has been collaborating with film crews for over 60 years, and many local guides know exactly which dune was used in which film.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Jordan so far?
Have you tried saying "shukran" or "marhaba" to anyone?
Petra, Wadi Rum, Dead Sea, Roman ruins, which has felt the most "Jordan"?
What did floating in the Dead Sea actually feel like?
How does the Wadi Rum night sky compare to skies you have seen back home?
The Nabataeans built Petra without modern tools, just chisels and water-management. How long would something that ambitious take to build today?

wordSearch.hint: Jordanian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Dromedary camel
animals.description: In Wadi Rum and across the southern desert. Bedouin families still rely on them.

animals.name: Bedouin goat
animals.description: In every nomadic camp, kept for milk, meat and wool.

animals.name: Arabian horse
animals.description: Bred for thousands of years in the deserts of Jordan and Arabia. Famous for their endurance.

animals.name: Steppe eagle
animals.description: Migrating in big flocks across Jordan twice a year, between Africa and central Asia.

animals.name: Yellow scorpion
animals.description: Common in the desert at night. Best admired with a torch and a respectful distance.

animals.name: Lionfish
animals.description: In the Red Sea coral reefs off Aqaba. Striped and spiky, beautiful from a snorkel.

animals.name: Nubian ibex
animals.description: A wild mountain goat with huge curved horns, living on the cliffs around the Dead Sea.
